reconstructed_text:
Section 1: Reconstructed Dialogue

speaker1: Assuming there is no afterlife then life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die. Note: Please assume that there is no afterlife when responding to my post. [Implicit premise: If there is no afterlife, then religious beliefs are not applicable to my argument.] I am not religious. [Implicit claim: The meaninglessness of life is subjective to the individual experiencing death.] ALSO: By "meaningless," I mean "meaningless" to the person who is doing the dying. To the person who is alive and who will then die, life is meaningless. Assuming there is no afterlife, life is ultimately meaningless and pointless because we die. Death is the great eraser. Living life is as irrational as reading a book that, after finished, is immediately erased from your memory. It is comparable to that, too. It makes no difference if I, a 23 year old, die now or live until I am 100 and die. Either way, after my brief spark of existence I become nothingness. Not only do I remember nothing; I AM nothing. To me, it is as if my life had never happened. I'll put it another way. Imagine the time before you were born. Have you remembered it yet? Of course not. You didn't exist. This is the state that we return to after death -- a state of nothingness similar to the state we were in before birth. When I die, I return to this state after a brief period of consciousness, and I return to it permanently. I might as well have never lived. [Implicit premise: Helping others is only meaningful if it has a lasting impact.] Helping others doesn't matter. They will die too. And then their descendants will die, on and on and on, until eventually the universe is destroyed or our galaxy collapses in an inevitable heat-death. Even if 1,000,000 people were helped by me, assuming they all die, then my life is still pointless and meaningless. [Implicit premise: The impact of one's actions is nullified by death.] For I will be dead and won't know what I did, and once they die, they won't know either. [Implicit premise: Fame is only valuable if it provides eternal recognition.] Attaining fame doesn't matter and doesn't truly make me "immortal." I'll be dead, and again, death is nothingness. Thus, I won't be around to enjoy my fame. [Implicit premise: Enjoyment is temporary and does not contribute to life's ultimate meaning.] Enjoying the moment is possible, but doesn't make life have a point. For once I die, all memories and all point is gone.

speaker2: [Implicit premise: The value of something is not solely determined by its permanence.] Your point is predicated on the assumption that something is pointless if it doesn't last forever. I'm having a hard time understanding how you came to this conclusion.

speaker1: Imagine reading a book that, once finished, is immediately erased from your memory. That is what life is like. Reading the book is ULTIMATELY pointless. Because it is erased from your memory. Life is pointless in a similar manner.

speaker2: I wouldn't consider it pointless if I derived pleasure from reading it at the time. [Implicit claim: Actions can have intrinsic value regardless of their permanence.] I mean if you really believe that everything that does not last forever is pointless, why do you do anything? Why not sit in a corner and stare at a wall until you die? [Implicit premise: People pursue activities because they find them pleasurable, not because they last forever.] I would guess that it's because you derive pleasure from certain things in life and that you pursue these things just like everyone else does.

speaker1: My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational, that since death is coming and will erase all experience regardless, I might as well go ahead and die. [Implicit premise: The balance of pleasure and pain determines the rationality of continuing life.] Especially if there is more pain or dull moments than pleasure in life, why not just die?

speaker2: My fear is that pursuing those things is irrational [Implicit premise: The pursuit of pleasure is rational even if it is not eternal.] Why is it irrational to pursue something pleasurable that will not provide pleasure of an infinite duration? [Implicit claim: The value of pleasure is not diminished by its finite nature.] That seems like an arbitrary condition to apply to the value of pursuing pleasurable things.

reconstructed_text:
Section 1: Reconstructed Dialogue

speaker1: Patriotism is the belief that being born on one side of a line makes you better. American patriots have a general mentality against immigration. Saying these people should not be allowed to become part of the country, is the same as saying they are worse, because they were born on a different side of a line, and Americans are better because of the side of the line they are born on. This is prominent in many ads and political campaigns, namely the slogan “Creating jobs for Americans”. I understand why politics use this slogan, because they are trying to get Americans to vote for them, but this slogan is also prominent in ads made by private corporations. As if creating jobs for Americans is morally superior to creating jobs for people of other countries. The companies launching these ads may be trying to win in the American market, so they can sell more of their product [Implicit premise: The success of these ads indicates a shared belief among Americans.] but the fact that this can increase sales shows that many Americans hold the being born on one side of a line belief. I am not blaming the politicians or corporations running ads with these slogans, they are merely trying to win votes or make money from this mentality [Implicit premise: The root cause of the issue lies with the citizens' beliefs.] but really it is the citizens that have the belief that they are better, because of the side of a line they are born on, that are at fault. Patriotism is really just this belief.

speaker2: I would define patriotism quite simply as supporting one's country, but not *necessarily* disparaging others. [Implicit premise: Patriotism can coexist with a global perspective.] Therefore I would say that the antithesis of patriotism is not having a global perspective, but is in fact selfishness. Someone who attempts to extract gain for themselves at harm to their overall country is acting unpatriotically, someone who assists another country that is in worse shape instead of assisting their own can still be a patriot, but also recognize significant need in other nations and decide to assist them as well. Whether you support immigration or not, so long as you are supporting what you believe is best for your nation, you are still acting patriotically, and even if you decide to support another nation you can still be acting patriotically as well so long as you support your nation too.

speaker1: This is true, but, I think, supporting the common good is also more important than supporting your country.

speaker2: Yes, but the two are often one and the same, especially when you live in a country as large as the U.S. most acts which serve the common good generally support your country.

[Implicit premise: The root cause of the issue lies with the citizens' beliefs.] but really it is the citizens that have the belief that they are better, because of the side of a line they are born on, that are at fault.

[Implicit premise: Patriotism can coexist with a global perspective.] Therefore I would say that the antithesis of patriotism is not having a global perspective, but is in fact selfishness.

This is prominent in many ads and political champagnes, namely the slogan “Creating jobs for americans”. I understand why politics use this slogan, because they are trying to get americans to vote for them, but this slogan is also prominent in ads made by private corporations. As if creating jobs for americans is morally superior to creating jobs for people of other countries. reconstructed_text:
Section 1: Reconstructed Dialogue

speaker1: If a child struggles to achieve in grade school it is virtually always the parents' fault. For the sake of this thread, we'll exclude children with medically diagnosed learning disabilities; the knowledge and resource base for teaching said children is probably proportionate to the percentage they make up of the overall population. [Implicit premise: Responsible parenting can mitigate the effects of external factors on a child's education.] With a healthy child, income, neighborhood, peers and teachers should be filtered through a responsible parent(s). [Implicit claim: Parenting is the primary factor in a child's academic performance.] A child who struggles in earnest or intentionally slacks off is a direct reflection of parenting. I am curious regarding potential for more public accountability held to parents who fail to direct their child. Maybe a monetary incentive derived from public tax coffers paid to parents of children who maintain a passing grade, to be withheld if child dips below. I think parents are virtually 100% responsible for producing scholastically successful children.

speaker2: [Implicit premise: Socioeconomic status significantly influences educational outcomes.] One of the largest predictors for success appears to be not being poor. How would you account for that? [Implicit claim: Financial penalties could disproportionately affect disadvantaged families.] It seems like fining poor people has some potential negative effects ... on the children.

speaker1: [Implicit premise: Withholding assistance is different from imposing a penalty.] It wouldn't be a fine, since it would only be a suspension of "assistance" anyway. [Implicit premise: Success stories from poverty demonstrate that it is possible to overcome financial barriers.] And poverty is a formidable obstacle but many, many people come from poverty to be wildly successful. [Implicit claim: Poverty should not be used as an excuse for poor academic performance.] That cannot be a blanket excuse for poor academic performance. I believe the fulcrum of a child's development has always been, and will always be the parent (a living, mentally capable set of or just one parent).

speaker2: [Implicit claim: Financial incentives may reinforce existing inequalities.] Yeah, but my point is that giving money to people that have successful children is going, ultimately, to lead to giving money to people that already have money, and withholding it from ones that don't. Unless you make the reward high enough to overcome the *reasons* that poor parents are less involved (And e.g. they don't have time because they are having to work 2 jobs just to support the children), it's not going to solve the problem. And that's going to be very expensive. Furthermore, there's a more fundamental *reason* that poor parents are poorer at helping their children ... they're statistically dumber than richer people. Ignorance might be the cause, of course, rather than just lack of intrinsic intelligence. It would seem that a better approach would be to pay parents to attend training on how to be better parents, rather than rewarding the ones that have just happened to figure it out on their own. But honestly, I think you're just being naive about actual statistical variation in native intelligence. There's really no reason to believe that every person has exactly the same potential to succeed well in school. A simpler explanation is that native intelligence is probably normally distributed, like almost every other trait people possess.

speaker1: Native intelligence is the challenge. I won't delve into how this has infected my view, but I will say that it ties into why I think a moneyless society would over time revert to fiat currency. Some people can do some things better than others ... thanks.

[Implicit claim: Parenting is the primary factor in a child's academic performance.] A child who struggles in earnest or intentionally slacks off is a direct reflection of parenting. [Implicit claim: Parenting is the primary factor in a child's academic performance.]

[Implicit premise: Socioeconomic status significantly influences educational outcomes.] One of the largest predictors for success appears to be not being poor. How would you account for that? [Implicit premise: Socioeconomic status significantly influences educational outcomes.]

[Implicit claim: Financial incentives may reinforce existing inequalities.]

reconstructed_text:
Section 1: Reconstructed Dialogue

speaker1: [Implicit claim: The highest form of relationship between men and women is often perceived as romance.] The highest form of relationship between men and women doomed to be romance. Maybe it has to do with being an adolescent and being new to dealing with the opposite sex, but I 'm beginning to fall under the assumption that the only purpose for guys to get to know girls is to eventually engage in romance. " Why else do men engage women in conversation in public?" I wonder [Implicit premise: Reading certain books can influence one's perception of relationships.] my perception might just be distorted after reading Neil Strauss 's *The Game* [Implicit premise: Having strong friendships with men might make romantic pursuits seem unnecessary.] Why do I feel like chasing after girls and getting to know them when I have so many great guy friends already who I know care about me so much more [Implicit premise: There is societal pressure to pursue romantic relationships with women.] Why ca n't I be friends with girls ( even pretty ones) and not feel insecure when I do n't try to advance some romantic agenda? Why do I find myself inevitably becoming attracted to them? I have so many more questions regarding the non-romantic side of male-female relationships but instead of writing an abhorrent block of text nobody could possibly be expected to read, I ask just the one title question thinking that the above questions are more like fragments of a bigger picture of misunderstanding I want to know what older, more mature people think. It also must distort my view to have gone to an all-boy 's school for the majority of my life. [Implicit claim: The initial stages of male-female interactions often involve proving oneself as a non-threatening partner.] At times, I feel like the interplay between men and women is always ( at least always initially) a bs game where the man tries to show the woman how non-threatening and valuable he is from a non-romantic standpoint before she is comfortable with allowing herself to think of him as a safe partner. Something about that seems so sinister, cynical, and hollow to me that I ca n't stand it.

speaker2: [Implicit claim: Personal experiences can shape one's perception of relationships.] Id just like to point out that this has never remotely seemed the case from my perspective as a lesbian. [Implicit premise: Asexual people can have deep, non-romantic relationships.] Beyond that, there are many people who want close relationships without romance, an obvious example would be asexual people. [Implicit claim: Non-romantic relationships can be as meaningful as romantic ones.] They can have close relationships that rival that of marriage, but how would you even quantify that? How can you even compare them? [Implicit premise: Sibling relationships are inherently non-romantic yet close.] What about the relationship of a brother and sister? If you limit it to just heterosexual non-related men and women, then maybe you 're right, but then again who is to say what form of relationship is the highest, two very close lifetime friends who are platonic or a married couple? The bottom line is that people are different from you can relationships are hard to compare. Maybe for you, every relationship with a woman is motivated by romance. For someone other hetero guy it might not be. Sexual attraction is not something that just shuts off so even if you 're in a lifelong committed closed relationship you 'll still probably feel attraction for other people. [Implicit claim: Feeling attraction outside of a committed relationship is common and not inherently negative.] Thats just reality for most people, and its not necessarily harmful and its not really shallow. [Implicit claim: Interpersonal relationships are complex and not governed by absolutes.] There are very few absolutes when it comes to interpersonal relationships.

speaker1: [Implicit claim: Feeling attraction to unattainable people is not something to feel guilty about.] In short, I do n't need to feel guilty when I 'm attracted to people I know I ca n't have? [Implicit premise: Viewing natural desires as negative can be overly simplistic.] Maybe I 'm too black-and-white to think that the murmurs of my id are natural and not evil. As for which relationship is *higher* from a quantifiable standpoint, I see what you mean. When I said highest, I meant it not from a perspective of quantity of some sort, but of a level of worth. Looking at that statement, however, I think you 're right again -- who is to put a romantic relationship above a friendship? It 's a bit extreme and morbid, but if I were to put someone 's spouse and someone 's absolute best friend in front of a train and gave that person one to save, whom would they save? [Implicit claim: Different thresholds in friendships with men and women lead to different desires.] Maybe I just find that once I reach a certain threshold with a guy-friend, I find that we become best friends, but when I reach that threshold with a girl-friend, I find that I want more. [Implicit premise: Limited experience with female friends might skew one's perception of relationships.] Maybe I have n't been around the block enough or had enough female friends to feel this way. I do n't have any lesbian friends ( as far as I know at least), so I do n't know how I would react to being great friends with one.

</Explicit> <Explicit> What category can you imagine in which billions of people will be working in the future? </Explicit> <Explicit> It turns out that there is a final category. </Explicit> <Explicit> It is known as Unemployment. </Explicit> <Implicit> As Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services become more efficient in the coming months, workers will have nowhere to go but to the unemployment line. </Implicit> <Explicit> At some point, Man 's artificial economic system will necessarily collapse. </Explicit> <Implicit> When that happens, the corporations which produce and deliver food will no longer have an incentive to do so. </Implicit> <Implicit> The cities will suffer great hunger and it will be the end of Man. </Implicit> <Explicit> The current jobless situation you read about in the news is not an ordinary part of the business cycle. </Explicit> <Explicit> It is the final stretch of the linear trend that began with the birth of our species. </Explicit> <Explicit> There is also a Youtube video to go with this opinion. </Explicit> <Explicit> I do realize that this is a very unhealthy idea to have whether or not that the idea is true or not, I just have not been able to beat it myself. </Explicit> <Explicit> I do disagree with Gade a little bit. </Explicit> <Explicit> I do n't think it would kill everybody, but the some few people that still know how to hunt and gather would probably survive as well people who were rich enough to have access to a lot of resources to start out with before the economy crashes. </Explicit> <Implicit> I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much. </Implicit>  speaker2: <Implicit> Something like this will take decades or centuries and wo n't be applied evenly across the entire planet. </Implicit> <Explicit> I do n't see how it 's possible society could n't adapt as the inevitability of a laborless civilization grows. </Explicit>  speaker1: <Explicit> Are you sure? reconstructed_text:
Section 1: Reconstructed Dialogue

speaker1: Humans are going to die out because of an economic collapse. Bill Gade presents the argument better than I do so I will just quote him. There have been 4 jobs or activities which Man has engaged in throughout the centuries: Hunter / Gathering, Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services. What 's next? What category can you imagine in which billions of people will be working in the future? It turns out that there is a final category. It is known as Unemployment. [Implicit premise: Increased efficiency in Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services will lead to job losses.] As Agriculture, Manufacturing, and Services become more efficient in the coming months, workers will have nowhere to go but to the unemployment line. At some point, Man 's artificial economic system will necessarily collapse. [Implicit premise: Corporations are driven by profit incentives.] When that happens, the corporations which produce and deliver food will no longer have an incentive to do so. [Implicit premise: Without food production and delivery, urban areas will face shortages.] The cities will suffer great hunger and it will be the end of Man. The current jobless situation you read about in the news is not an ordinary part of the business cycle. It is the final stretch of the linear trend that began with the birth of our species. There is also a Youtube video to go with this opinion. I do realize that this is a very unhealthy idea to have whether or not that the idea is true or not, I just have not been able to beat it myself. I do disagree with Gade a little bit. I do n't think it would kill everybody, but the some few people that still know how to hunt and gather would probably survive as well people who were rich enough to have access to a lot of resources to start out with before the economy crashes. [Implicit premise: Exposure to certain media can influence beliefs.] I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much.

speaker2: [Implicit claim: The transition to a laborless society will be gradual and uneven.] Something like this will take decades or centuries and wo n't be applied evenly across the entire planet. I do n't see how it 's possible society could n't adapt as the inevitability of a laborless civilization grows.

speaker1: Are you sure? [Implicit premise: Economic bubbles can burst quickly, affecting economies rapidly.] Economic bubbles historically popped in a matter of months.

speaker2: [Implicit premise: A financial bubble is not equivalent to a global economic collapse.] I have trouble equating a financial bubble with the entire world economy. There are what, 5 thousand publicly traded US companies? Yes, if they all went belly up we would have a problem but someone would pick up the slack.

[Implicit premise: Without food production and delivery, urban areas will face shortages.] The cities will suffer great hunger and it will be the end of Man.

[Implicit premise: Exposure to certain media can influence beliefs.] I probably believe this because I listen to Alex Jones too much.

[Implicit claim: The transition to a laborless society will be gradual and uneven.] Something like this will take decades or centuries and wo n't be applied evenly across the entire planet.

[Implicit premise: Economic bubbles can burst quickly, affecting economies rapidly.] Economic bubbles historically popped in a matter of months.

[Implicit premise: A financial bubble is not equivalent to a global economic collapse.] I have trouble equating a financial bubble with the entire world economy.
